1: The tenth tôlĕḏôt formula ("This is the written account of Adam's family line") opens the antediluvian genealogy with a deliberate echo of Genesis 1:26-27 — "When God created mankind, he made them in the likeness of God." Despite the Fall of Genesis 3, the ṣelem/dĕmût (image/likeness) language is explicitly restated as still operative. The image of God in humanity was not obliterated by sin; Genesis 9:6 will confirm it remains the basis for the prohibition of murder. The genealogy begins with the reaffirmation of the divine image because it will trace a line through fallen, mortal humanity to a flood — and the image-bearers who die throughout are not merely expendable statistics.
2: The summary of Genesis 1:27 — "he created them male and female and blessed them" — restates the creation mandate within the genealogical framework, establishing that the antediluvian genealogy is the fulfillment of the fruitfulness blessing. "He named them 'Mankind' (ʾādām)" — God's naming of the human race as a whole echoes the naming-as-sovereignty pattern of Genesis 1 (vv5,8,10). The collective name ʾādām encompasses all humanity in one named entity before the genealogy differentiates individuals — every individual in the list participates in the collective divine image-naming.
3: The genealogical formula reaches its theological core in v3: Adam's son Seth is described as being born "in his own likeness, in his own image" — the same vocabulary (dĕmût / ṣelem) used for humanity's creation in the divine image (Genesis 1:26). The theological implication is dual: the image of God is transmitted generationally, but through a fallen parent. What Seth inherits is both the divine image-bearing dignity and the fallen nature of post-Eden humanity. Augustine's doctrine of original sin finds its foundational text here: children inherit the likeness of their parents, including the spiritual consequences of the Fall.
4: The formula "Adam lived 800 years and had other sons and daughters" is the genealogy's standard secondary notation. The reference to "other sons and daughters" confirms that Adam's family was large, explaining the population presupposed in Genesis 4 (Cain's fear of being killed; Cain building a city). The genealogical register omits these other children — only the direct covenant-line is traced. This selectivity is characteristic of ANE and biblical genealogies: the purpose is to trace a specific lineage, not to document an entire family tree.
5: The refrain "and then he died" (wayyāmot) sounds for the first time in v5 and will repeat for every patriarch in the list (except Enoch, v24). The death-refrain is the genealogy's structural heartbeat: each life, however long, ends with the fulfillment of the death-warning of Genesis 2:17. The extreme lifespans — Adam 930 years — have close parallels in the Sumerian King List, which records antediluvian kings reigning for 28,800 to 36,000 years (clearly symbolic or astronomical numbers). The Genesis lifespans are dramatically shorter than the Sumerian parallels but follow the same pattern: extremely long pre-flood lives followed by dramatically shortened post-flood lives. Whether the numbers are literal, representative of tribal/dynastic generations, or use a different counting system remains debated.
6: The formulaic genealogical entry for Seth traces the direct covenant-line from Adam. Seth's 105-year lifespan before Enosh's birth is part of the decreasing-with-each-generation pattern that characterizes the genealogy — the antediluvian patriarchs' first-born ages at fatherhood are variable but their total lifespans cluster between 895 and 969 years. The names in the Sethite line (Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech, Noah) were preserved in Jewish tradition as the great pre-flood righteous figures; several appear in later Second Temple literature as bearers of divine revelation.
7: The secondary formula "Seth lived 807 years and had other sons and daughters" follows the genealogy's standard pattern for the years after the birth of the named heir. The genealogy's focus on the named covenant-line child while acknowledging other offspring follows the ANE practice of selective genealogy — the genealogy serves to establish a specific line of descent, not to census the entire population. The total lifespan of 912 years places Seth within the top tier of antediluvian longevity.
8: Enosh's name (ʾĕnôš) is a synonym for ʾādām — both mean "human being/man," with ʾĕnôš carrying a connotation of frailty and mortality (cf. Psalms 8:4: "What is ʾĕnôš that you are mindful of him?"; Job 4:17: "Can a mortal/ʾĕnôš be more righteous than God?"). The name at this point in the genealogy — just after the beginning of formal worship (Genesis 4:26: "at that time people began to call on the name of YHWH") — creates an implicit tension: the frail, mortal human creature is the one who calls on the eternal God.
9: Kenan's name (Qênān) may be related to Qayin (Cain) — pointing to the shadowy doubling between the Cainite and Sethite genealogies noted by scholars. The parallel names in the two genealogies (Enoch/Enosh, Irad/Jared, Methushael/Methuselah, Lamech/Lamech) suggest either a common name-pool or a deliberate structural mirroring. The genealogy's purpose is not to solve this puzzle but to establish the unbroken succession of the covenant-seed line from Adam to Noah.
10: Enosh's 815 post-Kenan years and total 905-year lifespan follow the genealogy's standard decreasing secondary pattern. The gradually shifting numbers across the genealogy — each patriarch's first-birth age and total lifespan are individually specified — suggest the numbers were carefully transmitted and not simply repeated. Ancient readers familiar with Mesopotamian genealogical lists would have found the specificity reassuring: the pre-flood lineage was a documented record, not a vague tradition.
11: The formulaic "Altogether, Enosh lived a total of 905 years, and then he died" marks the second sounding of the death-refrain. In the context of the genealogy's overall structure, these deaths are not narrative tragedies but theological punctuation marks — each life, however long, is bracketed by birth and death, confirming the mortality-within-dignity of the image-bearing creature. The repetition also creates a liturgical-like rhythm that ancient oral recitation would have reinforced: the genealogy was likely recited in formal contexts, its regular cadence part of its religious function.
12: Kenan's 70-year lifespan before Mahalalel's birth is the shortest first-birth age in the genealogy (the others range from 65 to 187 years). Mahalalel's name means "praise of God" or "God is praised" (māhalal = praise + ʾēl = God) — the first theophoric name in the Sethite genealogy, embedding a theology of praise directly into the covenant-line's naming practice.
13: The secondary formula for Kenan — 840 years after Mahalalel's birth — places Kenan's total at 910 years, in the central range of the antediluvian patriarchs. The function of the genealogy in its ANE context was to establish the antiquity and legitimacy of the covenant-line: the extreme lifespans are evidence of a blessed and divinely-sustained lineage reaching back to creation itself. No other ancient tradition could claim an unbroken line of such length — the genealogy is a covenant-identity document.
14: The death of Kenan at 910 years is the fourth sounding of the genealogy's death-refrain. The cumulative effect of four deaths in close succession (Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan) establishes an irresistible pattern before the exception of Enoch in v24 will shatter it. The narrative architecture is deliberate: the reader is being conditioned by repetition to expect "and then he died" so that "he was no more, because God took him" will register as the rupture it is.
15: Mahalalel's 65-year lifespan before Jared's birth is the second-shortest first-birth age in the genealogy, matching Enoch's age at Methuselah's birth (v21). Jared's name may derive from yārad (to descend) — the name is sometimes connected in later Jewish tradition with the "descent" of the Watchers (the angelic beings of Genesis 6:1-4) during Jared's lifetime, as developed in 1 Enoch. The connection is speculative but illustrates how later interpreters read the genealogy as encoding the timeline of the supernatural events that led to the flood.
16: Mahalalel's secondary lifespan of 830 years (total: 895 years) places him at the lower end of the antediluvian patriarchs' longevity. The systematic recording of these secondary years — years lived after the birth of the covenant-line heir — serves a chronological function: the genealogy allows ancient readers to calculate the total span from Adam to the flood, which the internal Genesis chronology places at 1,656 years. This kind of precise chronological genealogy is paralleled in Mesopotamian king-lists, which similarly serve as chronological anchors for understanding the relationship between the present and the primordial past.
17: The death of Mahalalel at 895 years is the fifth death-refrain in the genealogy. The cumulative death-toll before Enoch creates an unmistakable theological meditation on mortality: even the longest lives in human history all end the same way. The rabbinic tradition of reading Genesis 5 as a meditation on human transience and the need for righteous living within finite time reflects the text's own insistence: no matter how long the patriarch lived, "and then he died" concludes the account.
18: Jared's 162-year lifespan before Enoch's birth is one of the longer first-birth ages in the genealogy (only Methuselah at 187 is longer). The name Jared (Yereḏ) — from the root "to descend" — embedded the tradition of cosmic descent within the genealogical name. 1 Enoch, the Second Temple Jewish pseudepigraphon attributed to the next figure in the line, explicitly names the Watchers' descent during the days of Jared (1 Enoch Genesis 6:6), suggesting an early interpretive tradition that saw Jared's era as the era of the supernatural intermarriage described in Genesis 6:1-4.
19: Jared's 800 post-Enoch years give him a total of 962 years — the second-longest lifespan in the genealogy after Methuselah's 969. The secondary-years formula for each patriarch establishes the genealogy's double function: tracing the covenant-line (the named firstborn) and documenting the extended family (other sons and daughters). The "other sons and daughters" clauses are a consistent reminder that the genealogy is selective: the covenant-line is one thread in a much larger family tapestry.
20: Jared's death at 962 years is the sixth death-refrain, one before the genealogy's structural climax. The narrative rhythm has now established the unbroken pattern so firmly that the next entry — Enoch's — will break it with maximum impact. The reader's expectation of "and then he died" is the literary preparation for "God took him." The genealogy is building to its theological apex through deliberate, repetitive conditioning.
21: Enoch's 65-year lifespan before Methuselah's birth is the shortest first-birth age in the genealogy (tied with Mahalalel at v15). The name Enoch (Ḥănôḵ) means "dedicated/initiated" — the same name as Cain's city in Genesis 4:17, the first entry in the Cainite line. The Sethite Enoch mirrors and contrasts the Cainite city: the city in Cain's line represents civilization built apart from God; the person Enoch in Seth's line represents the closest possible human walk with God. The identical name creates a structural contrast between the two lines' representatives of human development.
22: Enoch's post-Methuselah life is described uniquely: he "walked faithfully with God 300 years." The Hebrew wayyithallek Ḥănôḵ ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm (Enoch walked with God) uses the same Hithpael form as YHWH's "walking in the garden" in Genesis 3:8 — the intimate garden-communion that Adam forfeited is recovered by Enoch. "Walked with God" is applied to only two antediluvian figures: Enoch (Genesis 5:22-24) and Noah (Genesis 6:9). In the ANE context, the figure of the apkallu (sage) who lived before the flood and enjoyed special divine knowledge was well-established; Enoch functions as Israel's equivalent — a sage who walked in divine intimacy without the mythology of divine-human ancestry.
23: Enoch's total lifespan of 365 years is conspicuously the shortest in the genealogy and numerically equal to the solar year. The solar symbolism has not been lost on interpreters ancient or modern: Enoch lived one year for every day of the year. Whether this is coincidence or deliberate numerology is unknown. What is certain is that the brevity of his years against the backdrop of the genealogy's 895-969-year lifespans heightens the paradox of his translation: the one who walked most closely with God lived the fewest years of any patriarch in the list.
24: The genealogy's structural rupture: "Enoch walked faithfully with God; then he was no more, because God took him away (wayyiqqaḥ ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm)." The death-refrain is absent — this is the genealogy's sole exception after seven identical entries. "God took him" uses the same verb (lāqaḥ) as Elijah's later translation (2 Kings 2:3,5,9-10: "YHWH is going to take your master from you today"). In Second Temple Judaism, Enoch became the premier figure of heavenly-journey apocalyptic literature: 1 Enoch (preserved in full in Ethiopic) elaborates his heavenly visions, his role as cosmic scribe, and his encounters with divine secrets. Hebrews 11:5 cites his translation as an act of faith; Jude 14-15 quotes from 1 Enoch, showing the tradition's currency in NT-era Judaism.
25: Methuselah's 187-year lifespan before Lamech's birth is the longest first-birth age in the genealogy. The name Methuselah (Mĕtûšelaḥ) may mean "man of the javelin/dart" or, in a popular etymology, "his death shall bring" — which, if intentional, would be consistent with Methuselah's death in the year of the flood according to internal chronological calculation (he lived 969 years; the flood came 600 years after Noah's birth in Lamech's 777th year). The internal chronology of the genealogy appears to have been constructed so that Methuselah dies precisely in the flood year — though without surviving the flood.
26: Methuselah's 782 post-Lamech years give him the genealogy's longest total lifespan: 969 years. The numerical specificity across the genealogy — no two patriarchs share identical first-birth ages or total lifespans — suggests intentional differentiation rather than schematic repetition. Ancient readers would have noticed Methuselah's record-setting longevity; the phrase "old as Methuselah" has entered English as the proverbial expression of extreme age. The genealogy's numbers were clearly transmitted as significant and distinguishable, not merely as round-number approximations.
27: Methuselah's death at 969 years is the record lifespan in the genealogy and the last death-refrain before the anomalous Enoch (whose death the formula skips). The regularity of "and then he died" through eight entries (Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared, Methuselah, Lamech) brackets the Enoch exception and resumes after it, confirming that Enoch's non-death was the intended structural highlight. Death is the universal human condition; Enoch's exception is the exception that proves the rule — and provides a glimpse of what the curse's reversal would look like.
28: Lamech's 182-year lifespan before Noah's birth continues the pattern of relatively long first-birth ages in the later generations of the genealogy. The naming of Noah in v29 will carry the genealogy's most explicit messianic-expectation language: the patriarch whose name means "rest/comfort" is given explicit eschatological freight by his father's hope. In the ANE context of the Sumerian King List, the flood-hero (Ziusudra/Utnapishtim) similarly stands at the end of a long pre-flood genealogical sequence before the flood narrative begins — Genesis employs the same structural architecture while filling it with theologically distinctive content.
29: Lamech's naming of Noah — "He will comfort us in the labor and painful toil of our hands caused by the ground the LORD has cursed" — is the genealogy's only extended comment, signaling the naming as theologically weighted. The wordplay: Noah (Nōaḥ) from nāḥam (to comfort/rest) or nûaḥ (to rest) — the root that gives us the Sabbath-rest (nûaḥ) and the "resting place" of the Ark (Genesis 8:4: the ark "rested" on Ararat). Lamech's hope that Noah will "comfort us" against the curse of 3:17 is a proto-messianic expectation: Noah will not ultimately remove the curse, but his covenant-relationship with YHWH will create the new-creation conditions of the post-flood world (Genesis 9:1-17).
30: Lamech's 595 post-Noah years are notably shorter than his forebears' secondary years, reflecting the genealogy's overall pattern of gradually declining secondary lifespans as the flood approaches. Total lifespan: 777 years — a symbolically significant number (seven is the number of completeness; 777 is a tripling). In contrast to Lamech of Cain's line (Genesis 4:23-24) — whose song boasted of 77-fold vengeance — this Lamech dies at 777 years and names a son who will bring comfort. The two Lameches are the genealogies' paired representatives: violence vs. comfort, vengeance vs. rest.
31: Lamech's death at 777 years precedes the flood by five years (if Noah was 600 at the flood, and Lamech died when Noah was 595). The death-refrain's return after Lamech confirms that the Enoch exception was the only exception: even the father of the flood-hero dies in the ordinary human way. Noah alone, like Enoch before him, will experience an extraordinary divine preservation — not translation but covenant-deliverance through the waters.
32: Noah's fathering of Shem, Ham, and Japheth at "after he was 500 years old" closes the Sethite genealogy with the three sons who will repopulate the earth after the flood. Unlike every previous patriarchal entry, Noah's is not followed by a secondary lifespan and death notice here — instead Genesis 6 opens immediately with the events leading to the flood. The three sons will become the ancestors of the Table of Nations in Genesis 10: Shem (ancestor of Semitic peoples, including Israel), Ham (ancestor of African and Canaanite peoples), Japheth (ancestor of Indo-European peoples). The genealogy that began with one man (Adam) ends with one man's three sons about to become humanity's new source.
